text:
False data injection attacks (FDIAs) are considered as one of the critical threats for smart grid systems. To address this challenge, the objective of this paper is to formulate the attack detection as a statistical learning problem. The existing detection methods depend on state vector estimation (SVE) technique that observes the measurement residual. The attacks which are invisible for SVE are called unobservable attacks; the attacker injects his own data in the column space of measurement Jacobian matrix. To tackle these attacks, this paper examines the performance of smart grid based on various machine learning techniques including supervised learning, semi-supervised learning, and online learning by classifying legitimate and compromised measurements.

We present a learning-based approach toward Secure Vector Evaluation (SVE) that leverages the strengths of various machine learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), $k$-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN), and Sparse Logistic Regression in conjunction with decision-level and feature-level fusion approaches like AdaBoost and Multiple Kernel Learning (MKL). Through an analysis of the geometric and statistical characteristics of measurement vectors, we show that, even in scenarios where an attacker is able to launch unobservable attacks and blind the traditional SVE solutions, our learning-based approach is capable of pinpointing such attacks and effectively preventing them from entering the vulnerability analysis framework. Simulation results obtained on IEEE 9-bus, 57-bus, and 118-bus test systems validate the superior performance of our learning-based solutions in terms of detection rates, accuracy, and true positive rate. Interestingly, we found that, in our SVM-based solutions, detection rate shows a phase transition behavior near the theoretically proven minimum number of attacks that an attacker has to commit so as to remain undetected. Our batch and online learning-based solutions are demonstrated to not only preserve the vulnerability analysis framework from the impact of unobservable attacks but also significantly enhance the security and robustness of the physical layer of smart grids.
